preamble: The shift from the postalveolar affricate [tʃ] to the voiceless palatal stop [c] is a process of deaffrication. It occurs when the fricative release is dropped, leaving only the stop closure in the palatal region. This is observed in certain historical sound changes where affricates simplify to plain stops before front vowels.

examples.note: In certain conservative dialects, the palatal lateral or fricative may reanalyze as a stop; not a standard shift but attestable in older forms.

sources: Hualde, J. I. (2009). The Sounds of Spanish.
Penny, R. (2002). A History of the Spanish Language.
